text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

The Paris 2024 Olympics mascots represent freedom and have a long history in France

The countdown is on to theParis 2024 Olympicopening ceremony.

Rather than the typical stadium setting, more than 10,000 athletes representing 206 countries will participate on a river tour of Parisian landmarks, including theNotre Dame cathedraland Pont Neuf.

Team GB’s joint flag bearers are diverTom Daleyand rowerHelen Glover, who have won three gold medals between them at previous Games.

The ceremony will also include French presidentEmmanuel Macronofficially opening the Games and the lighting of the Olympic cauldron with the Olympic flame.

Fans can look forward to 16 days of elite sport across 32 disciplines.

Breakdancingwill also make its official debut at the Games, after a successful appearance at the 2018 Youth Olympics.

Surfing, skateboarding and sport climbing will also be on display after being added to the Olympic programme for the first time at the2020 Tokyo Games.

The opening ceremony for Paris 2024 will start at 6.30pm BST tonight. British viewers can watch on BBC and Discovery+.

The Games will officially be launched at 7.24pm BST.

The mascots for the Paris 2024 Olympic mascots are the Phryges.

These cute red characters are actually based on the famous Phrygian cap, a symbol of freedom that has been part of French history for centuries and dates back to ancient times.

The Phrygian cap was widely popularised by French revolutionaries and is now a familiar image in France and associated with freedom, revolution and the French Republic.

It can be seen on the French national icon, Marianne, depicted in busts at town halls, on stamps and is also covered in the national curriculum in schools.

The Phyrgian cap is not new to major events in French history. The French National Archives also shows records of Phrygian caps worn during the construction of the city’s Notre Dame cathedral in 1163, during the revolution of 1789, during work to build theEiffel Towerand during the Paris 1924 Olympic Games.

This year’s mascots include an Olympian Phryge and aParalympianPhryge.

Each has their own personality and character.

According to the official website, the Olympic Phryge is a “thoughtful and an astute strategist” who “embarks on adventures only after carefully weighing up all the pros and cons”.

It continues: “Just like the Olympic athletes, she knows the importance of measuring all the various parameters to achieve her goals.

“With her sharp mind, she is modest and prefers to hide her emotions. The Olympic Phryge will lead the movement of all those who take part in sport, and believe us, she will give her all to get France moving!”

The Paralympic Phryge proudly wears a running prosthetic which enables her to run at lightning speed.

“Her passion is to blaze a trail; some might say she is fearless, which might be true, but one thing is certain: she hates being bored and loves to try new things.

“No matter the sport, and regardless of whether she competes as part of a team or on her own, she is always game to play.

“With her, you will get moving, playing, dancing, and sweating! With her mindset of being the perfect supporter, she loves promoting the values of sport, celebrating athletes in all arenas and all the Games venues, and partying to celebrate victories or get over defeats.”

Olympic mascots have been a key part of the Games since 1968.

According to the official Olympics website, they’re tasked with giving concrete form to the Olympic spirit, spreading the values highlighted at each edition of the Games; promoting the history and culture of the host city; and giving the event a festive atmosphere.

They are often an animal native to the area or human figures who represent the cultural heritage of a particular place.

The first Olympic mascot was born at the Grenoble Olympic Games in 1968. It was named “Schuss” and it was a little man on skis, designed in an abstract form and painted in the colours of France: blue, red and white.

The first official Olympic mascot appeared in the 1972 Summer Olympics in Munich.

Waldi theDachshund dog, a popular breed in Bavaria, represented the attributes required for athletes: resistance, tenacity and agility.

Håkon and Kristin were the official mascots of the 1994 Winter Olympics held in Lillehammer,Norway.

A boy and a girl, both dressed in Viking clothes, they were the first mascots to be human figures.

Izzy was the official mascot of the Atlanta 1996 Summer Olympics.

The animated character with the ability to morph into different forms was a departure from the Olympic convention in that it did not represent a nationally significant animal or human figure. It was the first computer-generated mascot in Olympic history.

In 2012, the Summer Olympics came to London.

The UK mascot was Wenlock, a drop of steel with a camera for an eye. It was named after the village of Much Wenlock in Shropshire, which hosted a precursor to the modern Olympic Games in the 19th century. Mandeville was the mascot of the 2012 Paralympics.

The mascots received a mixed reception with one critic describing them as the product of a “drunken one-night stand between a Teletubby and a Dalek”.

title: Who are the Paris 2024 Olympic mascots? Inspiration behind the Phryges
author: Joanna Whitehead
description: The Paris 2024 Olympics mascots represent freedom and have a long history in France
